title: Fattoush (Lebanese Bread Salad)

ingredients.name: stale or toasted flatbread or bread, broken into shards
visual_checks: pita, khubz or even regular bread works; leftover bread is ideal
ingredients.notes: pita, khubz or even regular bread works; leftover bread is ideal

subs.name: broken papad or poori shells — gives a similar crispy texture
subs.notes: changes the flavour profile but still works

subs.name: all coriander leaves for a more Indian-tasting salad
subs.notes: flavour changes but the concept holds

ingredients.name: amchur or sumac powder
visual_checks: sumac is traditional; amchur gives the same sour fruity note
ingredients.notes: sumac is traditional; amchur gives the same sour fruity note

subs.name: extra lemon juice (1 tbsp)
subs.notes: sumac has a fruity quality lemon alone cannot replicate, but amchur comes closest

ingredients.name: oil for shallow-frying bread (optional)
visual_checks: fry the bread pieces instead of toasting for extra crunch
ingredients.notes: fry the bread pieces instead of toasting for extra crunch

steps.text: Break or tear the flatbread into rough 3–4 cm shards. Either toast under a grill, in a dry pan on medium flame, or shallow-fry in 2 tablespoons oil for 2 minutes until golden and very crisp. Set aside — they should crunch loudly when pressed.

Prepare the vegetables: cut tomatoes into wedges and salt them lightly in a colander for 5 minutes, then gently squeeze to remove excess juice. Dice the cucumber and slice the spring onions and capsicum.
Make the dressing: whisk together lemon juice, olive oil, amchur (or sumac), and a pinch of salt in a small bowl until slightly emulsified.
Combine the tomatoes, cucumber, spring onion, capsicum, parsley and mint in a large bowl. Pour over the dressing and toss well.

Add the toasted bread pieces and toss once — then serve immediately. The bread should be crisp: if you dress the salad and wait, the bread softens. This is unavoidable, so assemble right before eating.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: bread → broken papad or poori shells — gives a similar crispy texture (changes the flavour profile but still works); amchur → extra lemon juice (1 tbsp) (sumac has a fruity quality lemon alone cannot replicate, but amchur comes closest); parsley → all coriander leaves for a more Indian-tasting salad (flavour changes but the concept holds)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Regular bread or chapati broken and toasted in a dry tawa makes an excellent substitute for pita. Amchur powder (dried mango) is the perfect Indian replacement for sumac. The salad needs no cooking — just chop, dress and serve. Works brilliantly as a side with any grilled dish.
